text: The heritability of gains in VO2max after a standardized 20-week exercise program is approximately 47%.

A panel of 21 SNPs accounts for 49% of the variance in VO2max trainability, indicating that roughly half of the individual variation in aerobic fitness response to training is explained by genetic factors.

Individuals carrying 9 or fewer favorable training-response alleles improved VO2max by only 221 ml/min, whereas those carrying 19 or more favorable alleles gained 604 ml/min from the same training program.
The strongest single genetic predictor of VO2max training response is rs6552828 in the ACSL1 gene, which alone accounts for approximately 6% of the training response variance.

Genetic high responders to exercise training can improve VO2max nearly three times as much as low responders (604 vs 221 ml/min) when following an identical standardized program, meaning coaches should expect wide individual variation in aerobic adaptations even with identical training.
specific_value: ~2.7x difference between high and low genetic responders

text: Several genetic predictors of VO2max trainability were replicated across independent cohorts including sedentary overweight/dyslipidemic subjects and sedentary obese women, suggesting these genetic influences on training response are robust across populations.
